title: PanelForge: Director-in-the-Loop AI Comic Studio
one_liner: A fully local AI comic studio where an LLM "director" scripts the story, an SDXL+IP-Adapter pipeline draws the panels, and a resident vision-LLM critic rejects and re-renders any panel where characters go off-model — turning a one-line premise into a polished, character-consistent comic page.
concept: PanelForge turns a one-sentence premise ("a warehouse robot secretly learns to paint") into a finished multi-panel comic page, entirely on one RTX 3090. What makes it more than "LLM writes a prompt": generation is a closed loop. First, qwen3.5:27b produces a structured Story Bible (characters with canonical visual descriptors, a 6-8 panel shot list with camera angles, dialogue, and per-panel SDXL prompts) as schema-validated JSON. The LLM is then evicted from VRAM and a custom diffusers pipeline takes over: each character gets a reference sheet, and every panel is rendered as 3 fast SDXL-Lightning draft candidates conditioned on the character refs via IP-Adapter. A small multimodal critic (gemma4:e4b, which fits in VRAM *alongside* SDXL) inspects each draft against the panel script — wrong jacket color, missing prop, broken framing — and either accepts a candidate or emits a revised prompt/seed/IP-weight, looping up to 2 rounds before a final high-step render. The whole LLM-vs-diffusion negotiation streams live into a "Director's Log" in the UI, and the user can click any panel and type a natural-language note ("make it rain here") that the LLM translates into a targeted re-render. embeddinggemma indexes the Story Bible and past pages so multi-page stories stay continuity-consistent. It is a genuine bidirectional LLM-diffusion system that demos spectacularly in 2 minutes.
core_features: One-click premise-to-comic: type a logline + pick an art style, get a complete 6-8 panel page with speech bubbles, captions, and gutters, exportable as PNG/PDF/CBZ
Character consistency engine: auto-generated character reference sheets (re-rollable) drive IP-Adapter conditioning in every panel, with a live DINOv2 cosine 'consistency score' badge per character

Director's Log: a streaming side panel showing the critic VLM rejecting drafts ('Mira's jacket is blue, canon is red; reframe low-angle') and the pipeline re-rendering until accepted — the feedback loop made visible
Click-to-edit panels: select a panel, write a note in plain English, and the LLM converts it into a surgical prompt/seed edit that re-renders only that panel while preserving identity
Multi-page continuity via RAG: embeddinggemma retrieves the Story Bible and prior-page summaries so page 2 remembers what happened on page 1
Draft-vs-final quality tiers: 4-step SDXL-Lightning candidates for the critique loop (~1.5 s each), 25-step SDXL final renders for accepted panels
llm_component: Three local Ollama models, each with a distinct role. (1) Planner: qwen3.5:27b with Ollama structured outputs (format=JSON schema) + Pydantic validation/retry, prompt-engineered as a film director — emits the Story Bible: characters {name, canonical visual descriptor, palette}, style guide, and per-panel {action, camera, dialogue, SDXL prompt, negative prompt}. Run with keep_alive=0 so it fully vacates VRAM after planning. (2) Critic/Art Director: gemma4:e4b (9.6 GB, multimodal) stays VRAM-resident next to SDXL; for each panel it receives the downscaled draft images + the panel script + character ref thumbnails and returns {verdict, defect list, revised prompt, ip_adapter weight delta} — the core of the feedback loop. It also translates user panel notes into prompt edits. (3) RAG: embeddinggemma embeds Story Bible entries and page summaries into a local Chroma store; the planner retrieves them for multi-page continuity. Techniques: prompt engineering with few-shot panel scripts, JSON-schema constrained decoding, agentic critique loop, lightweight RAG.
diffusion_component: Custom diffusers pipeline around stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0 (fp16, madebyollin/sdxl-vae-fp16-fix, VAE tiling). Character consistency via IP-Adapter: h94/IP-Adapter ip-adapter-plus_sdxl_vit-h (+ ip-adapter-plus-face_sdxl_vit-h for close-ups) with the laion CLIP-ViT-H image encoder, conditioning every panel on the character reference sheet; for two-character panels, diffusers ip_adapter_masks assign each identity to a region. Inference acceleration: ByteDance/SDXL-Lightning 4-step UNet LoRA toggled on for draft candidates in the critique loop, off for the 25-step DPM++ final render; fixed per-panel seeds make critic-driven and user-driven edits minimally invasive. Stretch goal: diffusers/controlnet-canny-sdxl-1.0 to lock panel composition from a rough layout sketch.
tech_stack: Python 3.11 in a fresh conda env (safest wheel coverage for the CUDA stack)
torch 2.5.x + cu121, diffusers >= 0.31, transformers >= 4.46, accelerate, safetensors

Models: stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0, ByteDance/SDXL-Lightning (sdxl_lightning_4step_lora), h94/IP-Adapter (SDXL vit-h plus + plus-face), madebyollin/sdxl-vae-fp16-fix, facebook/dinov2-small (consistency metric)
Ollama (already installed) via the official ollama-python client: qwen3.5:27b, gemma4:e4b, embeddinggemma
gradio 5.x (Blocks API, streaming log via generators), pydantic v2 for LLM JSON schemas, chromadb for the continuity store
Pillow + a bundled comic font for speech-bubble/gutter compositing; img2pdf for CBZ/PDF export
vram_plan: Two-phase budget with no co-residency of the big LLM and SDXL. Phase A (planning, ~20 s): qwen3.5:27b alone uses ~17 GB; called with keep_alive=0 so Ollama evicts it immediately after the Story Bible is returned. Phase B (rendering loop, persistent): SDXL fp16 + IP-Adapter + ViT-H image encoder peaks ~11-12 GB at 1024x1024 (VAE tiling + fp16-fix VAE keep decode spikes down), and gemma4:e4b stays resident at ~9.6 GB with a long keep_alive — total ~21-22 GB, leaving ~2 GB headroom (PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True; drafts rendered at 832x1216 portrait to trim activation memory; critic sees 768px downscales). Fallback toggle in config: "sequential mode" sets keep_alive=0 on the critic too and uses enable_model_cpu_offload, trading ~2x latency for guaranteed fit. A VRAM gauge in the UI footer makes the orchestration part of the demo.
build_plan: M1 (day 0.5): Environment + smoke tests — conda env, requirements.txt, single-script proofs: SDXL+IP-Adapter render on the 3090, qwen3.5:27b JSON-schema Story Bible via ollama-python, gemma4:e4b image-input sanity check (this de-risks the whole design on day one)

M2 (day 1): Planning chain — Story Bible Pydantic schemas, few-shot director prompt, retry-on-invalid-JSON loop; character reference sheet generation with re-roll; keep_alive=0 VRAM handoff verified with nvidia-smi logging

M3 (day 2): The core loop — panel renderer with Lightning-LoRA draft candidates, gemma4:e4b critic prompt returning structured verdicts, revision loop (max 2 rounds), final 25-step render, DINOv2 consistency scores
M4 (day 2.5): Compositor + editing — Pillow page layout with gutters, speech bubbles, captions; click-a-panel 'note to director' targeted re-render with preserved seed/identity; CBZ/PDF export
M5 (day 3): Gradio Blocks UI — premise/style inputs, character gallery, progressive page preview, streaming Director's Log, VRAM gauge; embeddinggemma+Chroma continuity store for page 2+
M6 (day 3.5): Deliverables — README with architecture diagram, agent-collaboration workflow log, 3 sample comics (one starring the lab's pi0 arm), record 2-minute demo video
risks: gemma4:e4b may handle image input poorly or not at all -> tested in M1; fallback A: critic becomes DINOv2/CLIP similarity gates (identity, prompt-image CLIPScore) + text-only LLM revision; fallback B: batch panels and swap qwen3.5:27b in for critique once per page (slower but works)

IP-Adapter identity bleed in multi-character panels -> planner is explicitly instructed in 'shot economy' (prefer 1-character shots, max 2); two-character panels use diffusers ip_adapter_masks regional conditioning; consistency score surfaces failures so the critic loop catches the rest

Co-resident VRAM spikes (SDXL VAE decode + 9.6 GB critic) -> fp16-fix VAE, VAE tiling, 832x1216 drafts, expandable_segments allocator, plus a one-flag sequential-offload fallback mode that always fits

Demo pacing: 6 panels x critique rounds could take minutes -> 4-step Lightning drafts (~1.5 s each on a 3090), panels stream into the page as they finish, and a 'fast demo' preset (4 panels, 1 critique round) guarantees a tight 2-minute recording
wow_factor: The demo opens with one typed sentence and ends 90 seconds later with a finished noir comic page being exported to PDF — but the hook is the middle: viewers watch the Director's Log as the critic VLM publicly rejects a draft ("Unit-7's chassis is the wrong color; canon is matte orange — reframe low-angle"), the panel visibly re-renders and fixes itself, and the character consistency score ticks up to 0.91. Then a live edit: click panel 3, type "make it rain", and only that panel regenerates with the same character and framing. Closing beat for the course audience: a second comic starring the lab's real pi0 robot arm, plus the VRAM gauge proving a 27B LLM, a 9.6B VLM critic, and SDXL all choreographed on a single 24 GB consumer GPU.

judge_verdicts.verdict: Through the grading rubric this is close to a model answer: the LLM and diffusion components are genuinely interlocked (LLM plans a schema-validated Story Bible, a resident VLM critic gates and revises diffusion outputs, the LLM translates user edits into surgical re-renders), and the diffusion side shows real technique depth — IP-Adapter plus face variant, regional ip_adapter_masks, Lightning LoRA draft/final tiering, fp16-fix VAE, tiling, seed-pinned edits, DINOv2 metrics. Deliverables are fully storyboarded (M6 explicitly covers README/architecture, agent-collaboration log, sample outputs, 2-minute demo), and the VRAM choreography itself demonstrates understanding most submissions never show. Neither technology is a wrapper; the only deduction is execution risk: the headline feature hinges on gemma4:e4b's untested image-input quality, and M1-M6 in ~3.5 days is ambitious enough that the compositor or multi-page RAG could ship half-baked.
judge_verdicts.improvement: De-risk the critic, the single point of failure for the headline feedback loop: make the deterministic gates (DINOv2 identity score + CLIPScore prompt-adherence thresholds) part of the main acceptance path from M3 onward — not just "fallback A" — with the gemma4:e4b VLM layered on top for natural-language defect reports. Then the Director's Log demo works even if the small VLM's image grounding proves flaky, and the M1 go/no-go test stops being a bet on the whole project.

judge_verdicts.verdict: This is an unusually well-engineered proposal: every named HF model and exact weight filename verified to exist, all three Ollama models are already pulled and gemma4:e4b confirms vision capability (the design's load-bearing assumption), Python 3.11 pinning correctly dodges the 3.13 wheel trap, zero training is required, and the day-0.5 smoke tests de-risk exactly the right things. The two-phase VRAM plan with keep_alive=0 and a one-flag sequential-offload fallback is genuinely sound. It loses points for optimistic numbers: the desktop already eats 832MB of VRAM, Ollama's runtime footprint exceeds its 9.6GB disk size, so co-resident Phase B is ~23-24GB on ~23.7GB usable (they will live in fallback mode more than claimed), and the 20s-planning / 90s-demo latency claims are 2-4x optimistic (27B Story Bible JSON is realistically 50-90s; full page 4-7 min).
judge_verdicts.improvement: Make sequential mode (critic keep_alive=0 + enable_model_cpu_offload) the default and treat SDXL/critic co-residency as a measured optimization: the actual machine has only ~23.7GB usable (832MB consumed by gnome-remote-desktop/sunshine) and Ollama's resident footprint exceeds disk size, so the claimed 2GB headroom is near zero; cap the critic's num_ctx, call torch.cuda.empty_cache() between phases, and re-baseline the demo timing honestly around the sequential path so the recorded demo never hits a cross-process OOM.

judge_verdicts.verdict: This is one of the rare proposals where the demo is the architecture: watching a VLM critic publicly reject a draft ("jacket is blue, canon is red"), the panel visibly re-render itself, and a consistency score tick up is a genuine, visceral wow moment no plain SD wrapper has — and the click-a-panel "make it rain" live edit plus the pi0-robot-arm comic closer are perfectly pitched for a robotics-lab classroom. Demo pacing is honestly engineered (Lightning 1.5s drafts, streaming panels, an explicit 4-panel fast-demo preset, VRAM gauge as proof-of-orchestration), though the 90-second end-to-end claim is optimistic once you count the 27B load/evict and six 25-step final renders. It loses points because "AI comic generator" itself is a known genre (AI Comic Factory), SDXL+IP-Adapter pages with Pillow speech bubbles frequently look amateurish in practice, and the entire centerpiece moment depends on gemma4:e4b being a competent visual critic — its fallbacks (CLIP/DINO gates) keep the app alive but flatten the Director's Log into far less theatrical telemetry.
judge_verdicts.fatal_flaw: none — but conditionally close: the headline wow moment (an articulate on-camera rejection by the e4b critic) is probabilistic. If the small VLM hallucinates defects, accepts bad drafts, or fails image input entirely, the demo degrades from "AI art director argues with the painter" to a progress log, and the fallback paths don't recover the theater.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Make the rejection moment deterministic instead of hoping a defect occurs on camera: add a demo "sabotage" toggle that intentionally perturbs one draft's prompt (swap the canonical jacket color) so the critic reliably catches and corrects it in every take — and pre-warm the SDXL pipeline before recording so the qwen-to-SDXL VRAM handoff doesn't eat 30 silent seconds of the 2-minute video.

title: SceneForge — Type a Task, Forge a Labeled Robot-Vision Dataset
one_liner: Describe a robot manipulation task in plain English and an LLM scene director plus depth-ControlNet SDXL forge a photoreal, domain-randomized, auto-labeled training dataset in seconds — no simulator required.
concept: SceneForge is a generative domain-randomization factory for robot vision, inspired by Google's ROSIE and GenAug. The user types a task ("pick the red mug from a cluttered kitchen table"); a local LLM acts as a constrained scene director, emitting a validated JSON SceneSpec (objects, poses, camera) where abstract object requests are grounded to a real mesh library via embedding RAG. A lightweight headless renderer (trimesh+pyrender, no Isaac Sim) places collision-checked meshes and renders a depth map plus pixel-perfect instance masks. SDXL with a depth ControlNet, accelerated to 4 steps with SDXL-Lightning, then re-imagines that exact geometry across LLM-generated style worlds (kitchen, garage, lab, outdoors). The killer insight: because ControlNet preserves the rendered geometry, the renderer's ground-truth masks and bounding boxes remain valid on every photoreal variant — labels come for free. One click exports a COCO dataset, and an OWLv2 spot-check reports a label-fidelity score, making this a genuinely useful tool for the user's own pi0/OpenVLA fine-tuning work while centering course DGM tech (prompt-engineered agentic LLM + RAG + ControlNet + inference acceleration).
core_features: Natural-language task box: one sentence becomes a full 3D scene layout, previewed live in an interactive gr.Model3D viewer alongside the LLM's JSON SceneSpec
Same geometry, different worlds: a gallery grid where each row is one physical layout rendered photorealistically in 4+ LLM-invented style domains (2s/image via SDXL-Lightning)
Free ground-truth labels: toggleable bounding-box and instance-mask overlays on every generated image, derived from the renderer, not from a detector
One-click COCO export: zip with images, masks, COCO JSON, and the SceneSpecs — ready to feed a detector or VLA fine-tune
Label-fidelity meter: OWLv2 open-vocab detector spot-checks generated images against the labels and reports a per-batch fidelity score, auto-filtering drifted samples
RAG asset grounding: ask for 'something to drink from' and embeddinggemma retrieval resolves it to the closest mesh in the YCB/CC0 asset library
llm_component: gemma4:e4b (9.6GB, local via Ollama python client) as an agentic 'scene director': (1) structured-output generation (Ollama format=JSON-schema) of a pydantic-validated SceneSpec — object list, table-plane placements, camera azimuth/elevation; (2) a validate-and-repair loop: pydantic/collision errors are fed back to the LLM for up to 3 self-correction rounds; (3) prompt engineering to spin each layout into N diverse style-domain prompts (lighting, materials, environment) for the diffusion stage. RAG: embeddinggemma (621MB) embeds 'asset cards' for the ~30-mesh library; abstract object requests are grounded by cosine-similarity retrieval. Optional 'quality mode' uses qwen3.5:27b with keep_alive=0 (unloads before diffusion starts).
diffusion_component: Custom diffusers pipeline: StableDiffusionXLControlNetPipeline with stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0 (fp16) + diffusers/controlnet-depth-sdxl-1.0, conditioned on the pyrender depth map (conditioning_scale ~0.8-1.0) so generated images keep the exact scene geometry and renderer labels transfer. Inference acceleration via ByteDance/SDXL-Lightning 4-step UNet checkpoint (fallback: latent-consistency/lcm-lora-sdxl), VAE tiling + attention slicing, ~2s per 768-1024px image on the 3090. This hits two named course techniques: ControlNet conditioning and inference acceleration.
tech_stack: torch 2.5.x (cu124), diffusers >=0.31, transformers, accelerate, safetensors

Models: stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0, diffusers/controlnet-depth-sdxl-1.0, ByteDance/SDXL-Lightning (4-step UNet), latent-consistency/lcm-lora-sdxl (fallback), google/owlv2-base-patch16-ensemble (fidelity eval)
Ollama + ollama-python: gemma4:e4b (director), embeddinggemma (RAG), qwen3.5:27b (optional quality mode, keep_alive=0)
Scene engine: trimesh + pyrender (EGL offscreen, PYOPENGL_PLATFORM=egl), pydantic v2 for SceneSpec schema
Assets: YCB object meshes (robotics-standard) + Kenney/Poly Haven CC0 GLBs (~30 total, shipped in repo)
UI/export: Gradio 5.x Blocks (gr.Model3D, gr.Gallery), pycocotools, numpy/opencv for mask->bbox
vram_plan: Co-residency by design: gemma4:e4b stays loaded in Ollama (9.6GB) + SDXL fp16 UNet/ControlNet/text-encoders (~9-10GB with VAE tiling and attention slicing at 768-1024px) + embeddinggemma (0.6GB) = ~20GB peak on 24GB, leaving ~3-4GB headroom for activations (verified feasible; smoke test is build-day 1). OWLv2 (~1.2GB) loads only during eval after a torch.cuda.empty_cache(). Fallbacks if headroom shrinks: enable_model_cpu_offload() on the SDXL pipeline (125GB RAM absorbs it), or set Ollama keep_alive=0 so the LLM unloads during generation bursts. qwen3.5:27b (17GB) is restricted to the spec-only phase with keep_alive=0 — never co-resident with SDXL.
ui_mockup:
+--------------------- SceneForge ---------------------+
| Task: "pick the red mug from a cluttered table"      |
| [FORGE] layouts:3  styles:4  steps:4(Lightning) s:42 |
+---------------------------+--------------------------+
| 3D LAYOUT (depth ctrl)    | LLM SCENE SPEC (JSON)    |
|     ___                   | objs: mug(.31,.12)       |
|    |mug|  \screwdriver    |  bowl(.55,.40) drill..   |
|    |___|   \   (o)bowl    | cam: az 38 el 22 d 0.9m  |
|   ====== table ======     | styles: kitchen, garage  |
|                           |  lab_bench, outdoor      |
+---------------------------+--------------------------+
| DATASET  12 imgs  [x]bboxes [x]masks   [Export COCO] |
| [kit1][kit2][kit3][kit4]   <- same geometry,         |
| [gar1][gar2][gar3][gar4]      different worlds       |
| [lab1][lab2][lab3][lab4]                             |
+------------------------------------------------------+
| fidelity 93% (OWLv2) | VRAM 19.6/24G | 1.9 s/img     |
+------------------------------------------------------+
build_plan: M1 (day 1 AM): conda env + smoke test — install torch/diffusers/gradio, run SDXL-Lightning + ControlNet-depth at 768px while gemma4:e4b sits resident in Ollama; log nvidia-smi to confirm <21GB co-residency and lock the VRAM strategy

M2 (day 1 PM): scene engine — curate ~30 YCB/CC0 GLB assets with text 'asset cards'; trimesh collision-checked tabletop placement; pyrender EGL offscreen rendering of depth map + instance-ID masks; mask->bbox extraction
M3 (day 2 AM): LLM director — pydantic SceneSpec schema, Ollama structured-output prompt, validate-and-repair retry loop, embeddinggemma RAG index over asset cards, style-variant prompt generator
M4 (day 2 PM): diffusion stage — wire depth maps into StableDiffusionXLControlNetPipeline with Lightning UNet, batch styles x layouts, tune controlnet_conditioning_scale, COCO JSON + zip exporter
M5 (day 3): Gradio Blocks UI — task box, gr.Model3D layout preview, gallery with bbox/mask overlay toggle, export button, live VRAM/timing footer; end-to-end happy-path hardening
M6 (day 4): eval + deliverables — OWLv2 label-fidelity scorer with auto-filtering, README with architecture diagram, requirements.txt, agent-collaboration log, record 2-minute demo video
risks: Geometry drift: ControlNet-depth can hallucinate or displace objects, invalidating labels. Mitigate with conditioning_scale 0.8-1.0, high-contrast normalized depth maps, and the OWLv2 fidelity filter that discards drifted samples (also turns a weakness into a graded eval feature); stretch: stack a canny ControlNet for harder cases

VRAM contention between Ollama and SDXL. Mitigate by designing around gemma4:e4b (9.6GB) co-residency, verified in M1; fallbacks are enable_model_cpu_offload (125GB RAM) and Ollama keep_alive=0; qwen3.5:27b allowed only in a spec-only phase

Headless pyrender/EGL can be finicky. Mitigate with PYOPENGL_PLATFORM=egl (known-good on RTX 3090/Ubuntu), pin pyrender-compatible Python (3.11 env if 3.13 misbehaves); fallback to Open3D OffscreenRenderer for depth+masks

LLM emits invalid or physically silly layouts. Mitigate with pydantic schema validation, deterministic post-processing (snap objects to table plane, trimesh collision resolution), 3-round LLM self-repair loop, then a safe default layout so the demo never stalls
wow_factor: The demo reads like magic but is fully grounded: type one sentence, watch the LLM's JSON scene spec stream in, a 3D layout pops up in the viewer, and then a 3x4 grid of photoreal images of the SAME physical scene materializes at ~2s each across kitchen/garage/lab/outdoor worlds — flipping the bbox overlay on shows pixel-aligned ground-truth labels on every single variant, something a vanilla text-to-image model fundamentally cannot do. Close the video with one click exporting a 100+ image COCO dataset and the OWLv2 fidelity meter ticking to ~93%, then a line connecting it to the author's actual research: 'this is the data pipeline I'd feed my lab's pi0/OpenVLA policies.' It demonstrates LLM agents, RAG, structured generation, ControlNet, and diffusion acceleration in one coherent, useful artifact.

judge_verdicts.verdict: Through a grading lens this is near-ideal: both technologies are load-bearing, not decorative. The LLM does structured-output generation with a pydantic validate-and-repair loop, RAG grounding via embeddinggemma, and style-prompt synthesis; the diffusion side is a custom StableDiffusionXLControlNetPipeline hitting two explicitly named course techniques (ControlNet conditioning, inference acceleration via Lightning/LCM-LoRA). The deliverables story is complete and scheduled per-milestone (Gradio Blocks UI, COCO export, OWLv2 quantitative eval, README/requirements/agent-log/demo video), and the VRAM co-residency plan shows real systems understanding rather than naive API calling.
judge_verdicts.fatal_flaw: none — the closest candidate is that the project's central claim ("renderer labels stay valid on every diffusion variant") rests on an unproven assumption that ControlNet-depth retains tight geometric adherence under 4-step SDXL-Lightning sampling, where conditioning fidelity is known to degrade; if drift is severe the headline feature collapses into a filtered subset. The proposal acknowledges this and converts it into a graded eval (OWLv2 fidelity filter), so it is a real risk but not a dealbreaker.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Expand the M1 smoke test from VRAM-only to a quantitative go/no-go on the load-bearing assumption: render 10 depth maps, generate with Lightning-4-step ControlNet, and require mean IoU between renderer masks and OWLv2 detections above a threshold (e.g., 0.7); if it fails, fall back to LCM-LoRA at 8 steps or dual depth+canny ControlNets before building everything downstream. Secondary scope hedge: the non-generative scene engine (trimesh/pyrender/EGL, asset curation) is the schedule's biggest time sink — pre-commit to a fixed tabletop template and 15 assets as the minimum viable cut so UI/eval polish (the graded deliverables) never gets sacrificed.

judge_verdicts.verdict: This is unusually rigorous feasibility planning: every named HF model verified to exist and is battle-tested, zero training runs, downloads (~16GB) and disk (236GB free) check out, the VRAM contention problem is confronted head-on with a numbered budget, a day-1 smoke test, and two credible fallback chains (cpu_offload on 125GB RAM, keep_alive=0). The deductions are for quantitative optimism: the ~20GB co-residency estimate understates Ollama's KV-cache/CUDA overhead (~11GB real for the 9.6GB model) and SDXL+ControlNet activations at 1024px, so co-residency is only safely true at 768px; and 1.9s/img with ControlNet plus attention slicing is closer to 3-4s. The 4-day scope (renderer + agent + RAG + diffusion + eval + UI) is full but each component is well-trodden and the riskiest integration is front-loaded.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Make phase-sequential execution the default rather than the fallback: run the LLM director with keep_alive=0 so it unloads before each diffusion burst, lock generation at 768px, and pin the env to python=3.11 + numpy<2 from the start (unmaintained pyrender breaks on numpy 2.x / Python 3.13). Co-residency at 1024px should be the stretch goal demoed only if the M1 smoke test proves headroom, since the stated 20GB budget omits Ollama KV-cache and ControlNet activation overhead.

judge_verdicts.verdict: This is one of the rare course-project concepts with a real, demoable "only this app can do that" moment: flipping pixel-aligned ground-truth boxes/masks onto a 3x4 grid of photoreal images of the SAME scene across invented worlds is visceral, instantly legible to a professor, and categorically beyond any vanilla SD wrapper. The loop is fast enough to show live (4-step Lightning at ~2s/image means the grid fills in under 30s while the JSON spec and 3D layout give the audience something to watch), and the pi0/OpenVLA closing line lands as a credible research tie-in rather than a gimmick. It loses points only because the entire wow hinges on ControlNet actually preserving geometry — if overlays visibly misalign on screen, the demo's central claim deflates — and because 4-step Lightning under heavy depth conditioning tends to produce slightly soft, "rendery" images that won't match the aesthetic punch of a slower pipeline.
judge_verdicts.fatal_flaw: none — the closest thing is visible label/geometry drift from depth-ControlNet (the demo dies if a bbox sits next to the mug instead of on it), but conditioning_scale tuning plus the OWLv2 auto-filter make it a manageable risk rather than a dealbreaker, provided the demo uses curated, filter-passing batches.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Make the geometry-control claim interactive instead of batch-only: let the presenter grab the camera (or nudge an object) in the gr.Model3D viewer and hit re-forge, so in ~5 seconds the audience sees the photoreal image and its labels move in lockstep with the 3D edit. That single live manipulation proves "labels come for free because we own the geometry" far more viscerally than a static grid, and it doubles as insurance footage if any one batch shows drift. Secondarily, render the overlay toggle as an animated wipe in the video so the label alignment is unmissable at 2-minute-video resolution.

title: DiffLab Tutor
one_liner: A fully-local RAG tutor for diffusion/flow-matching papers that does not just cite the math — it tool-calls an instrumented Stable Diffusion pipeline to run the experiment and show you, live.
concept: DiffLab Tutor is a chat-with-the-canon study app for the Deep Generative Models course itself. It indexes ~18 canonical papers (DDPM, DDIM, Score-SDE, Latent Diffusion, Classifier-Free Guidance, Flow Matching, Rectified Flow, Consistency Models, LoRA, ControlNet, SDXL — plus Diffusion Policy and pi0 as a robotics-lab tie-in) using embeddinggemma for dense retrieval and answers with qwen3.5:27b via Ollama, citing verbatim equations with paper/section/page references. The twist that makes the diffusion model integral rather than decorative: the LLM is an agent with a registry of 'lab tools' built on an instrumented diffusers SD1.5 pipeline. Ask 'what does the guidance scale w actually do?' and it retrieves Ho & Salimans Eq. 6, explains it, then calls cfg_sweep() and embeds a fixed-seed 4-image grid at w=1/3/7/13 in the chat. Ask about the forward process and it renders q(x_t|x_0) at increasing t; ask about samplers and it runs DDPM vs DDIM vs DPM++ at matched seeds and step counts; ask about flow matching and it trains a tiny rectified-flow MLP on 2D moons in ~20 seconds and animates straight ODE trajectories next to curved diffusion paths. Every figure ships with the exact diffusers code that produced it. It is a tutor that performs the lecture's experiments on demand — a perfect thematic fit for a DGM capstone, and every component runs locally on the 3090.
core_features: Paper-grounded Q&A over 18 canonical DGM papers with hybrid retrieval (embeddinggemma dense + BM25, reciprocal-rank fusion) and a clickable Sources panel showing the verbatim retrieved passage, equation, and page reference

'Run the experiment' agent: the LLM tool-calls instrumented SD1.5 lab demos — CFG scale sweeps, DDPM/DDIM/DPM++ sampler-and-step ablations at fixed seed, forward-noising visualizations of q(x_t|x_0), denoising-trajectory filmstrips of predicted x0 captured via step callbacks, and latent slerp interpolations
Live 2D flow-matching lab: trains a small rectified-flow MLP on toy data on the GPU in ~20 s and returns an animated trajectory plot when the question is about flow matching vs diffusion
Reproducibility panel: every generated figure displays the exact diffusers code, scheduler, seed, and parameters used, with a copy button
LaTeX-rendered chat (Gradio latex_delimiters) so retrieved equations like the CFG combination rule display as real math
VRAM dashboard plus automatic orchestration: live meter, preflight memory check before each lab run, and one-click swap to a high-quality SDXL-Turbo mode that auto-switches the LLM to gemma4:e4b
llm_component: qwen3.5:27b via Ollama (python client) as a tool-calling agent: per turn it decides between answer-only, retrieve-then-answer, or retrieve + lab-tool call, emitting tool arguments via Ollama structured outputs (JSON schema) for reliability. RAG stack: embeddinggemma (Ollama /api/embed) dense vectors in a persistent ChromaDB store + rank_bm25 keyword index, fused with reciprocal rank fusion; answers are grounded with chunk IDs so citations map to real retrieved text. gemma4:e4b (9.6 GB) is the drop-in low-VRAM fallback model used in SDXL-Turbo mode.
diffusion_component: Custom instrumented diffusers pipelines, used as the teaching apparatus: stable-diffusion-v1-5/stable-diffusion-v1-5 (fp16, ~4 GB) with callback_on_step_end to capture intermediate latents/predicted-x0 for denoising filmstrips; interchangeable schedulers (DDPMScheduler, DDIMScheduler, DPMSolverMultistepScheduler) for sampler ablations; direct scheduler math + VAE for forward-noising demos; latent slerp for interpolation demos. Optional high-quality figure mode uses stabilityai/sdxl-turbo (1–4 steps, ~8 GB). Plus a from-scratch 2D rectified-flow/flow-matching trainer (small PyTorch MLP) for FM questions.
tech_stack: Python 3.13 (fresh conda env), PyTorch 2.7 + CUDA 12.x
diffusers >=0.34, transformers, accelerate, safetensors
HF models: stable-diffusion-v1-5/stable-diffusion-v1-5 (fp16), stabilityai/sdxl-turbo (optional hi-res mode)
Ollama models (already pulled): qwen3.5:27b (chat + tools), gemma4:e4b (fallback), embeddinggemma (retrieval); ollama python client >=0.5
RAG: chromadb >=1.0 (persistent local store), rank-bm25; ingestion via arxiv package + ar5iv HTML (BeautifulSoup4) with pymupdf4llm PDF fallback
UI/visuals: gradio >=5.x (Blocks, Chatbot with latex_delimiters), matplotlib + imageio for trajectory and flow-matching animations
vram_plan: Primary config needs no swapping: qwen3.5:27b resident via Ollama (~17 GB, keep_alive=30m) + SD1.5 fp16 pinned in torch (~2.5 GB weights, ~4 GB peak at 512px batch-4) + embeddinggemma (0.6 GB) ≈ 21.5 GB of 24 GB; the 2D flow-matching MLP is negligible. A preflight torch.cuda.mem_get_info check runs before every lab call. SDXL-Turbo mode (~8 GB) triggers orchestration: either evict the 27B with a keep_alive=0 request and reload after, or auto-switch chat to gemma4:e4b (9.6 GB + 8 GB ≈ 18 GB, no swapping). Last-resort degradation: enable_model_cpu_offload on the diffusion pipeline (125 GB CPU RAM available).
ui_mockup:
+------------------------------------------------------+
| DiffLab Tutor      [18 papers indexed]  VRAM 21/24GB |
+--------------------------+---------------------------+
| CHAT                     | LAB: cfg_sweep (running)  |
| You: what does guidance  | "astronaut riding horse"  |
|  scale w actually do?    | w=1   w=3   w=7   w=13    |
| Tutor: Ho & Salimans'22  | [img] [img] [img] [img]   |
|  Eq.6: e=(1+w)ec - w eu  | seed=42 SD1.5 DDIM 25stp  |
|  sharpens p(x given c)   | > show diffusers code     |
|  fig -> running lab...   +---------------------------+
| [cites: CFG p.3, LDM p5] | SOURCES (retrieved)       |
|                          | [1] CFG sec3  [2] LDM s4  |
+--------------------------+---------------------------+
| Ask> [______________________________________] [Send] |
| try: DDIM vs DDPM @10stp / forward noising / pi0 FM  |
+------------------------------------------------------+
build_plan: M1 (day 0.5): conda env + torch/diffusers/gradio install; corpus fetcher pulls ~18 papers (ar5iv HTML preferred, PDF fallback), section-aware chunking, embeddinggemma + ChromaDB + BM25 indexing; CLI retrieval sanity test
M2 (day 1): RAG chat loop with qwen3.5:27b — system prompt, RRF retrieval, grounded citations with chunk IDs, LaTeX rendering in a minimal Gradio Chatbot

M3 (day 2): lab toolkit in diffusers — forward_noising, denoise_trajectory (step callbacks), cfg_sweep, sampler_comparison, latent_slerp, plus the 2D flow-matching trainer/animator; each returns a labeled grid or GIF + the code string used
M4 (day 2.5): agent layer — Ollama tool calling with JSON-schema structured outputs routing questions to retrieval and/or lab tools; keyword-router fallback; results embedded in chat
M5 (day 3): full Gradio Blocks UI — sources panel, lab panel with code accordion, preset question chips, VRAM meter, SDXL-Turbo mode with model orchestration; end-to-end testing on the 3090
M6 (day 3.5-4): README with architecture diagram, requirements.txt, agent-collaboration workflow log, record the 2-minute demo video
risks: Equation fidelity from PDFs is lossy -> ingest ar5iv/arXiv HTML (LaTeX preserved) as the primary source, pymupdf4llm fallback, keep equations verbatim in chunks and render via Gradio latex_delimiters
Local-model tool-calling flakiness -> small fixed tool registry, Ollama structured outputs with JSON schema validation + retry, deterministic keyword router as fallback so the demo never dead-ends

VRAM contention between the 27B LLM and the diffusion model -> default path (27B + SD1.5 = ~21.5 GB) verified at startup with a preflight check; auto-degrade ladder: keep_alive=0 eviction -> gemma4:e4b swap -> CPU offload

Demo latency -> SD1.5 at 512px / DPM++ 20 steps is ~1-2 s per image on a 3090 (a 4-image sweep lands in under 10 s), SDXL-Turbo needs 1-4 steps, pipelines stay pre-warmed, and preset question chips keep the 2-minute video tight
wow_factor: The demo reads as 'a tutor that runs its own lecture demos': ask about classifier-free guidance and within seconds the chat shows the retrieved equation rendered in LaTeX, cited to page 3 of the actual paper, followed by a live fixed-seed image grid where the astronaut visibly sharpens and then over-saturates as w climbs from 1 to 13; ask 'show me denoising' and a filmstrip of predicted-x0 emerges from pure noise; ask 'flow matching vs diffusion' and it trains a rectified flow on 2D moons live and animates straight ODE paths next to curved diffusion trajectories — all fully local on one RTX 3090, with the exact code for every figure one click away, and a pi0 flow-matching question as the robotics-lab mic-drop closer.

judge_verdicts.verdict: Rare proposal where both technologies are structurally load-bearing: the LLM (hybrid RAG + agentic tool-calling with structured outputs) dispatches to instrumented diffusers pipelines (step callbacks, scheduler ablations, latent slerp, from-scratch rectified-flow trainer), so removing either guts the app. Deliverables are complete and explicitly scheduled, the meta-fit to a DGM course is excellent, and hardware claims check out against the actual machine (models and 3090 verified). Deductions: all real-model diffusion work is inference-side with no LoRA/ControlNet/fine-tuning element, and the VRAM budget uses the 27B's 17 GB disk size, ignoring KV-cache/runtime overhead that likely pushes the 'no swapping' config past 24 GB.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Add one training-side diffusion lab tool — e.g., a tiny LoRA fine-tune on ~20 style images with a fixed-seed before/after grid the agent can invoke for 'how does LoRA work?' questions — which directly hits the rubric's named techniques (LoRA) and adds a fine-tuning element the current all-inference design lacks; secondarily, replace the 17 GB disk-size VRAM estimate with a measured runtime footprint of qwen3.5:27b (KV cache included) and make gemma4:e4b the default if the 27B + SD1.5 combo actually exceeds 24 GB.

judge_verdicts.verdict: This is an unusually buildable proposal: every named model verifiably exists (SD1.5 re-upload and sdxl-turbo on HF, all four Ollama models already pulled locally), the whole pip stack installs cleanly on Python 3.13 via cp313/abi3 wheels, there is zero training beyond a 20-second toy MLP, and the diffusion latency numbers check out on a 3090. The one engineering misstep is that the headline 'no swapping' VRAM budget (21.5 GB) is computed from disk sizes and omits KV cache, two CUDA contexts, and the ~0.8 GB desktop baseline measured on this machine — realistic load is 25-26 GB, so the primary config will silently partial-offload the 27B to CPU and slow the chat. The proposal earns the 8 anyway because it anticipates exactly this with a preflight mem_get_info check and a concrete three-rung degrade ladder, so the demo degrades gracefully rather than dying.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Fix the VRAM plan with runtime accounting instead of disk sizes: at startup, load qwen3.5:27b first, measure actual free VRAM with the SD1.5 pipeline warmed (expect ~25-26 GB demand vs 24 GB), and make gemma4:e4b the default chat model with qwen3.5:27b as an opt-in 'deep answer' mode that evicts via keep_alive=0 — otherwise the primary demo path runs with the 27B partially offloaded to CPU and chat latency (which dominates the demo loop far more than the 2-second SD renders) balloons to 30-60 s per answer.

judge_verdicts.verdict: This is one of the rare proposals where the demo audience-fit is perfect: a DGM professor watching a chatbot retrieve the actual CFG equation, render it in LaTeX with a page citation, then immediately run a fixed-seed guidance sweep on a live SD pipeline is a genuine wow moment, and the live-trained rectified flow with straight-vs-curved trajectory animation is a memorable, distinctive visual no SD-wrapper project will have. The interaction loop is demonstrably fast enough for a live demo (sub-10s sweeps, preset chips, pre-warmed pipelines), and the split chat/lab/sources UI concept is clean and screenshot-friendly. It loses points only because the raw pixels themselves (astronaut CFG grids, sampler ablations) are figures every diffusion tutorial has shown — the novelty is the agentic automation, not the imagery.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Make the centerpiece visceral by streaming, not batching: render the denoising filmstrip and sampler comparison live frame-by-frame in the lab panel as steps complete (a real-time DDPM-vs-DDIM 'race' where images visibly emerge from noise), and open the 2-minute video with that plus the flow-matching trajectory animation rather than a wall of chat text — static finished grids read as 'another diffusion notebook,' while live-forming images read as an instrument.

title: StoryReel — One-Prompt Illustrated Audiobook Studio
one_liner: Type a one-sentence story idea and a local LLM "director" scripts it into scenes, then SDXL-Lightning illustrates them, Stable Audio Open composes the score, and Kokoro narrates — exported as a finished 60-second mini-film, all on one RTX 3090.
concept: StoryReel is a local-only multimodal story studio: the user types a one-paragraph idea (e.g. "a lighthouse keeper adopts a tiny storm dragon"), picks a visual style and mood, and presses Forge. A local LLM director (gemma4:e4b via Ollama, structured JSON output) breaks the idea into 4-6 scenes, each with narration text, an SDXL image prompt, and a music brief — grounded by a RAG layer (embeddinggemma over ~100 curated "style cards" of cinematography and music-direction vocabulary) so prompts read like a professional art director wrote them. Two diffusion models then realize the script: SDXL-Lightning (4-step distilled UNet) renders style-consistent scene illustrations in ~2 s each, and Stable Audio Open (latent diffusion transformer) generates a 47 s stereo score; Kokoro-82M TTS narrates on CPU and is auto-ducked over the music. The user can chat-edit individual scenes ("make scene 2 stormier") — the LLM rewrites only that scene's fields and only the affected assets regenerate. It is compelling because both pillars of the course (LLM + diffusion) are load-bearing, the VRAM choreography on a shared 24 GB GPU is a real engineering story, and the output is an emotionally complete artifact, not a single image. A tasteful robotics nod: a "Lab Demo Trailer" preset turns a dry robot-experiment description into a cinematic project trailer.
core_features: One-prompt storyboard: LLM director emits a validated JSON StoryScript (per-scene narration, image prompt, music brief, mood arc) via Ollama structured outputs + Pydantic
RAG-grounded art direction: embeddinggemma retrieves style cards (shot composition, lighting, genre/instrumentation vocab) so image/music prompts are expert-level and consistent across scenes

Few-step scene illustration: SDXL-Lightning 4-step renders each scene at 1024px in ~2 s with global style presets (watercolor / noir / anime / storybook) and locked seeds for visual coherence; per-scene re-roll button
Diffusion soundtrack + narration mixdown: Stable Audio Open generates a 47 s stereo score from the LLM's music brief; Kokoro TTS narration is auto-ducked over it with pydub
Conversational scene editing: a chat box ('make scene 3 darker, minor key') makes the LLM patch only that scene's JSON, regenerating only the affected image/audio
One-click MP4 export: moviepy assembles images with Ken Burns pan/zoom + crossfades, synced to narration and score; all assets individually downloadable
llm_component: gemma4:e4b (9.6 GB, local via Ollama Python client) as the "director" agent. Techniques: (1) prompt engineering with few-shot scene-script examples; (2) Ollama structured outputs (format = Pydantic-derived JSON schema) producing a validated StoryScript, with one auto-repair retry loop; (3) RAG — embeddinggemma (621 MB, already pulled) embeds ~100 hand-curated cinematography/music-direction style cards, top-k cosine retrieval (numpy, no vector DB) injected into the director prompt; (4) an iterative edit agent that applies chat instructions as minimal JSON patches to single scenes. Optional "quality director" toggle uses qwen3.5:27b with keep_alive=0 so it vacates VRAM before diffusion starts.
diffusion_component: Two diffusion models, both integral. Images: SDXL-Lightning 4-step distilled UNet (ByteDance/SDXL-Lightning, sdxl_lightning_4step_unet.safetensors) loaded into stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0 via diffusers StableDiffusionXLPipeline with EulerDiscreteScheduler(timestep_spacing="trailing") — this is the course's "inference acceleration" technique (25x fewer steps), with madebyollin/sdxl-vae-fp16-fix for fp16 stability. Audio: stabilityai/stable-audio-open-1.0 (1.2B latent diffusion transformer) via diffusers StableAudioPipeline, 100 steps, up to 47 s stereo 44.1 kHz, driven by the LLM-composed music brief. Fallbacks if gating/VRAM bites: stabilityai/sdxl-turbo (images), facebook/musicgen-medium or declare-lab/TangoFlux flow-matching (audio).
tech_stack: Python 3.11 conda env (deliberately not 3.13 — broader wheel support for audio libs)
torch 2.5.x +cu121, diffusers >= 0.32, transformers, accelerate, safetensors

Models: gemma4:e4b + embeddinggemma (Ollama), ByteDance/SDXL-Lightning on stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0, madebyollin/sdxl-vae-fp16-fix, stabilityai/stable-audio-open-1.0, hexgrad/Kokoro-82M (kokoro pip pkg + espeak-ng, runs on CPU)
ollama Python client (structured outputs, keep_alive control), pydantic v2 for StoryScript schema
gradio 5.x (Blocks UI, streaming progress), pydub + soundfile for ducked mixdown, moviepy + ffmpeg for MP4 assembly
numpy cosine-similarity RAG store (style_cards.json), huggingface_hub for gated-model auth
vram_plan: Default budget: gemma4:e4b (9.6 GB) stays resident in Ollama the whole session (keep_alive=-1) for instant chat edits; SDXL-Lightning fp16 (~8.5-10 GB incl. text encoders/VAE) coexists with it at ~19 GB peak — safe on 24 GB. A GPUStage context manager enforces that at most ONE diffusion pipeline is resident: after the image phase, the SDXL pipe is deleted + torch.cuda.empty_cache(), then Stable Audio Open (~3-4 GB fp16) loads for scoring. Kokoro-82M runs on CPU (125 GB RAM is plentiful). If the user enables the qwen3.5:27b (17 GB) quality director, the app sets keep_alive=0 so Ollama evicts it before any diffusion load; a startup nvidia-smi check warns if >5 GB is already occupied. Last-resort fallback: enable_model_cpu_offload() on the SDXL pipe.
ui_mockup:
+----------------------------------------------------+
| StoryReel - One-Prompt Illustrated Audiobooks      |
+----------------------------------------------------+
| Idea: [A lighthouse keeper finds a storm dragon ]  |
| Style:[Watercolor v]  Mood:[Cozy v]  (Forge Story) |
+----------------------------------------------------+
| Scenes: [1*][2 ][3 ][4 ]    progress ###- 3/4 done |
| +------+ Narration: "Mira climbed the spiral       |
| |scene |  stair as thunder rolled below..."        |
| | img  | Music: warm strings, 70 bpm, hopeful      |
| +------+ [Re-image] [Re-write] [Re-score]          |
| chat> make scene 2 stormier, minor key      [Send] |
+----------------------------------------------------+
| Score     |=====waveform============| 47s   (Play) |
| Narration |===waveform====|  ducking: auto         |
+----------------------------------------------------+
| (Export MP4)   GPU: e4b 9.6G | SDXL hot | 19.2/24G |
+----------------------------------------------------+
build_plan: Day 1 AM — Environment + script brain: conda env (py3.11, torch/diffusers/gradio), accept Stable Audio Open license with HF token, define Pydantic StoryScript schema, get gemma4:e4b returning valid scene JSON via Ollama structured outputs with retry-repair

Day 1 PM — RAG art direction: write ~100 style cards (shot composition, lighting, music genre/instrumentation), embed with embeddinggemma, wire top-k retrieval into the director prompt; verify prompt quality on 5 test stories
Day 2 AM — Image stage: SDXL-Lightning 4-step pipeline with fp16-fix VAE, style presets + seed locking, plus the GPUStage load/unload manager with nvidia-smi logging
Day 2 PM — Audio stage: StableAudioPipeline scoring from music briefs, Kokoro CPU narration per scene, pydub ducked mixdown; benchmark end-to-end VRAM and latency
Day 3 — Gradio Blocks UI: idea form, storyboard scene cards with per-scene regen buttons, chat-edit box wired to JSON-patch agent, streaming progress, audio players
Day 4 AM — MP4 assembly: moviepy Ken Burns + crossfades synced to narration timing; 'Surprise me' and 'Lab Demo Trailer' presets; cache one pre-generated story for live demos
Day 4 PM — Deliverables: README with architecture diagram, pinned requirements.txt, agent-collaboration workflow log, record 2-minute demo video
risks: Stable Audio Open is gated on HF (license: 'other') — mitigate by accepting the license + HF token on day 1 before anything else; fallbacks already coded behind a flag: facebook/musicgen-medium (transformers, ungated) or declare-lab/TangoFlux (flow-matching)

VRAM collision between Ollama and diffusers — mitigate with the small e4b director by default, explicit keep_alive control via the Ollama API, a context manager guaranteeing one resident diffusion pipeline, startup nvidia-smi assertion, and enable_model_cpu_offload as last resort

LLM JSON drift breaking the pipeline — mitigate with Ollama schema-constrained structured outputs, Pydantic validation, one automatic repair retry, and few-shot examples; on double failure surface the raw text for manual edit instead of crashing

Demo latency / video-assembly scope creep — 4-step SDXL is ~2 s/image so a 5-scene reel completes in ~3 min, acceptable live; Ken Burns is a simple moviepy zoom with a static-slideshow fallback, and one cached story guarantees a smooth recording
wow_factor: The demo opens with a single typed sentence and a stopwatch: within ~3 minutes a storyboard fills in scene by scene, the presenter chat-edits one scene live ("stormier, minor key") and only that image and music cue regenerate, then Export plays a finished 60-second watercolor film with synchronized narration ducked under an original diffusion-composed score — all on one consumer GPU, with a corner overlay of nvidia-smi showing the LLM and the two diffusion models hot-swapping inside 24 GB. Closing beat: the "Lab Demo Trailer" preset turns a dry robotics-experiment paragraph into a cinematic trailer, landing the personal connection.

judge_verdicts.verdict: Both pillars are genuinely load-bearing: the LLM is a structured-output director with RAG grounding and an agentic JSON-patch editor (not a prompt-passthrough), and diffusion appears twice with rubric-named techniques (SDXL-Lightning distilled 4-step inference acceleration with correct details like trailing timestep spacing and the fp16-fix VAE, plus Stable Audio Open's latent DiT). The VRAM choreography (e4b resident + GPUStage one-pipeline-at-a-time + keep_alive control) and the explicit deliverables plan (README/architecture, pinned requirements, agent log, cached-demo video) show real systems understanding and a complete grading story. Deductions: all diffusion is inference-only off-the-shelf checkpoints (no LoRA/ControlNet/IP-Adapter), the "locked seeds = visual coherence" claim is naive for cross-scene character consistency, and there is a small design inconsistency between one global 47s score and per-scene "[Re-score]"/music-cue editing.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Replace the weak "locked seeds" coherence mechanism with IP-Adapter (or a quick style LoRA) on the SDXL-Lightning pipeline to keep the protagonist consistent across scenes — it directly hits a rubric-named technique the proposal currently lacks, fixes the most visible quality flaw in the output, and costs only ~1-2 GB of the VRAM headroom already budgeted.

judge_verdicts.verdict: Every named model verified to exist on HF including the exact Lightning UNet filename and the diffusers-format Stable Audio Open layout; the gated model is auto-approve and has two ungated coded fallbacks. VRAM, download, and latency arithmetic all roughly check out, the Python 3.13/pydub-audioop trap is deliberately sidestepped with a 3.11 env, and there is zero training anywhere — the 4-day plan front-loads the genuinely risky pieces. This is buildable in the stated time on this exact machine by a competent grad student with agent assistance.
judge_verdicts.improvement: The "~19 GB safe coexistence" claim undercounts Ollama overhead: gemma4:e4b's 9.6 GB file balloons to ~11-12.5 GB resident with KV cache and compute buffers, putting true peak at 20-23 GB. Make the default behavior evict e4b (keep_alive=0) during the SDXL render phase — reloading a 9.6 GB model costs ~5-10 s versus flirting with OOM/fragmentation mid-demo — and verify actual usage with `ollama ps` plus a torch.cuda.memory snapshot during the Day 2 PM benchmark.

judge_verdicts.verdict: This clears the "not another SD wrapper" bar: the demo artifact is a finished 60-second film with an original diffusion-composed score ducked under narration, and the live chat-edit beat ("stormier, minor key" regenerating one scene in ~2s while SDXL stays hot) plus the nvidia-smi VRAM-choreography overlay are genuinely visceral, professor-pleasing moments. The UI concept (storyboard cards, per-scene regen, chat box, waveforms, VRAM ticker) is clean and the cached-story fallback shows real demo discipline. It falls short of 8+ because the visual core carries two known risks: seed-locking alone will not keep the protagonist consistent across 5 SDXL scenes (the "mini-film" can collapse into a disconnected Ken Burns slideshow — the exact trope every AI-story-video tool produces), and the wow-factor script oversells music editing — there is one global 47s score, so "only that music cue regenerates" actually means a full 100-step re-render plus an SDXL-to-StableAudio pipeline swap, a 1-2 minute dead spot if attempted live.
judge_verdicts.fatal_flaw: none — though the claim that a chat edit regenerates "only that scene's music cue" is internally inconsistent with the single-47s-score design; if the presenter triggers a music edit live, the demo stalls on a full score regen plus a model swap. Must be scripted around or fixed.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Add a character-consistency mechanism — generate one "character sheet" image up front and feed it to every scene via IP-Adapter (or at minimum inject a fixed, LLM-frozen character description block into all scene prompts) so the lighthouse keeper is recognizably the same person in all 5 shots; secondarily, restructure the score as 4-6 short per-scene cues crossfaded together so the "re-score scene 2" edit is actually fast enough to show live.

title: ReRoom — AI Interior Design Studio
one_liner: Snap a photo of any room, get interviewed by an AI design consultant, and watch the room re-rendered in four different styles in seconds — with the exact wall/furniture geometry locked by a depth ControlNet.
concept: ReRoom is a photo-to-redesign studio that solves the real problem with text-to-image redecorating: vanilla img2img destroys room geometry, and most users can't write good SDXL prompts. ReRoom fixes both. A local LLM (gemma4:e4b via Ollama) acts as a design consultant that interviews the user in 3-4 chat turns (mood, materials, lighting, budget vibe), grounds its suggestions in a RAG knowledge base of ~80 curated interior-design style documents (Japandi, mid-century modern, wabi-sabi, brutalist...) embedded with embeddinggemma, and then emits a validated JSON design spec: prompt, negative prompt, ControlNet mode, and conditioning strength. The diffusion side extracts a depth map with Depth-Anything-V2 and runs SDXL + ControlNet-Depth accelerated by the SDXL-Lightning 4-step LoRA, producing a 2x2 grid of restyled rooms in ~8 seconds total while the room's structure stays pixel-faithful. A before/after slider and the LLM's written "design rationale card" make the result feel like a consultation, not a slot machine. Bonus tab for the robotics angle: feed in Isaac Sim screenshots and use the same depth-locked pipeline as a sim-to-photoreal restyler — same code path, free differentiator.
core_features: Chat interview: LLM consultant asks 3-4 targeted questions, then auto-writes the SDXL prompt + settings (user never writes a prompt)
Geometry-locked restyling: depth map auto-extracted from the photo; ControlNet-Depth preserves walls/furniture layout, with a Structure slider (conditioning scale 0.4-0.9)
Instant 2x2 style grid: 4 style variants in ~8s via SDXL-Lightning 4-step LoRA; one-click 'Upscale HQ' re-renders the favorite at 30 steps
Before/After comparison slider (gradio ImageSlider) + downloadable design rationale card written by the LLM
Canny mode toggle for line-faithful cases (sketches, floorplan elevations) and a style-reference upload via IP-Adapter (stretch goal)
Sim2Real bonus tab: restyle Isaac Sim renders into photorealistic scenes using the identical depth-ControlNet path
llm_component: gemma4:e4b (9.6 GB, already pulled) served by Ollama via the ollama-python client. Three techniques: (1) prompt-engineered multi-turn interview agent with a system prompt defining the consultant persona and question budget; (2) RAG — embeddinggemma embeds ~80 short style-knowledge docs (curated once, stored as a numpy matrix, cosine search) so the consultant cites real design vocabulary (materials, palettes, era references) instead of hallucinating; (3) structured output — final turn uses Ollama format=json constrained to a Pydantic schema {prompt, negative_prompt, control_mode, controlnet_scale, style_names[4], rationale}; validation failure triggers one automatic retry. The LLM is integral: it is the only way prompts and ControlNet settings get authored in the app.
diffusion_component: Custom diffusers StableDiffusionXLControlNetPipeline: stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0 (fp16) + madebyollin/sdxl-vae-fp16-fix + diffusers/controlnet-depth-sdxl-1.0 (toggle: diffusers/controlnet-canny-sdxl-1.0). Conditioning images from depth-anything/Depth-Anything-V2-Small-hf (depth) or OpenCV Canny. Inference acceleration: ByteDance/SDXL-Lightning sdxl_lightning_4step_lora.safetensors loaded via peft (4 steps, guidance 1.0-1.5, EulerDiscrete trailing timesteps) for the interactive grid; LoRA unfused for the 30-step HQ pass. Stretch: h94/IP-Adapter sdxl_models/ip-adapter_sdxl.safetensors for style-reference images. This hits three rubric keywords natively: ControlNet, LoRA, inference acceleration, inside a custom pipeline.
tech_stack: Python 3.13 conda env; PyTorch 2.5+ cu124; diffusers >=0.31; transformers; accelerate; peft; safetensors
stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0 + madebyollin/sdxl-vae-fp16-fix (fp16)
diffusers/controlnet-depth-sdxl-1.0 and diffusers/controlnet-canny-sdxl-1.0
ByteDance/SDXL-Lightning (4-step LoRA) for fast mode
depth-anything/Depth-Anything-V2-Small-hf + opencv-python for control image extraction
Ollama: gemma4:e4b (consultant) + embeddinggemma (RAG) via ollama-python; pydantic for JSON schema validation
Gradio 5.x (Chatbot, ImageSlider, Gallery components)
h94/IP-Adapter (SDXL) — stretch goal only
vram_plan: Resident on GPU the whole session: SDXL fp16 + one ControlNet + fp16-fix VAE ≈ 12.5-13.5 GB, Depth-Anything-V2-Small ≈ 0.1 GB, desktop ≈ 0.8 GB → ~14.5 GB steady state. The LLM is gemma4:e4b at 9.6 GB (NOT the 17 GB models): worst-case overlap 14.5 + 9.6 ≈ 24.1 GB, so we never let them overlap — Ollama is called with keep_alive="0" so e4b unloads immediately after each chat turn (e4b cold-loads in ~2-3 s, fine for chat pacing), and embeddinggemma runs once at startup to embed the KB then unloads. The workflow is naturally phase-separated anyway: interview first, generate after. Safety nets: torch.cuda.empty_cache() before each generation, and a --low-vram flag that switches the SDXL pipeline to enable_model_cpu_offload() (drops idle diffusion VRAM to ~4 GB, ~2x slower) using the 125 GB system RAM. The 17 GB qwen3.5/gemma4:26b models are deliberately unused.
ui_mockup:
+------------------------------------------------------+
| ReRoom Studio   Mode:[Depth v]  [Fast 4-step|HQ 30]  |

| YOUR ROOM        | DESIGN CONSULTANT (gemma4:e4b+RAG)|
| [ room photo   ] | AI: What mood — cozy, minimal,    |
| [ depth map    ] |     or dramatic?                  |
|                  | You: cozy japandi, warm wood      |
| Structure  0.65  | AI: Oak, linen, paper lanterns,   |
| [Upload] [Cam]   |     soft warm light. Rendering    |
|                  |     4 looks... [rationale card]   |
+------------------+-----------------------------------+
| RESTYLED — geometry locked by ControlNet-Depth       |
| +---------+ +---------+ +---------+ +---------+      |
| | japandi | | midcent | | wabisabi| | scandi  |      |
| +---------+ +---------+ +---------+ +---------+      |
| [<== Before / After slider ==>]  [Upscale HQ] [Save] |
+------------------------------------------------------+
build_plan: Day 1 AM — Pipeline spike: conda env, install torch/diffusers/gradio; standalone script proving SDXL + ControlNet-Depth + Lightning 4-step LoRA on one room photo; measure VRAM (~13.5 GB) and latency (~2 s/image) on the 3090
Day 1 PM — Control module: Depth-Anything-V2 depth extraction + OpenCV canny, cached per upload; Structure-slider wiring (controlnet_conditioning_scale); seed handling for the 2x2 grid

Day 2 AM — LLM consultant: Ollama chat loop with persona system prompt, keep_alive=0 VRAM policy, format=json final turn validated by Pydantic with one retry; build the ~80-doc style KB and embeddinggemma RAG retrieval (numpy cosine)
Day 2 PM — Gradio UI: two-column layout (image+controls left, Chatbot right), 2x2 Gallery, ImageSlider before/after, HQ re-render button, rationale card render
Day 3 — Stretch + bonus: IP-Adapter style-reference upload; Sim2Real tab fed with 2-3 Isaac Sim screenshots; canny mode for floorplan/sketch inputs

Day 4 — Hardening + deliverables: --low-vram offload path, error states, requirements.txt, README with architecture diagram, agent-collaboration log compiled from the dev sessions, record 2-min demo video (interview -> grid -> slider -> sim2real kicker)
risks: VRAM collision between Ollama and SDXL -> mitigated by using 9.6 GB gemma4:e4b (never the 17 GB models), keep_alive=0 so the LLM never sits resident during generation, and a tested enable_model_cpu_offload fallback flag

Lightning 4-step LoRA can degrade quality when combined with ControlNet (it was trained without it) -> grid uses Lightning at guidance 1.0-1.5 with conditioning scale <=0.7; the hero shot in the demo uses the 30-step HQ path with LoRA unfused, so quality is never gated on the fast path
LLM emits malformed or weak prompts -> Ollama format=json + Pydantic validation with auto-retry, plus a hand-written fallback prompt template per style so generation never blocks on the LLM

Scope creep (IP-Adapter, sim2real, canny mode are all tempting) -> core demo = depth mode + interview + grid + slider, done by end of Day 2; everything else is an isolated stretch tab that can be cut without touching the core
wow_factor: The 2-minute video tells a complete story with visible magic at each beat: (1) upload a real photo of the grad student's actual messy apartment/lab; (2) a natural chat where the AI consultant asks smart questions and replies with genuinely knowledgeable design language (RAG-grounded); (3) four fully restyled rooms appear in ~8 seconds — fast enough to feel live, not time-lapsed; (4) the before/after slider is the money shot: every wall, window, and couch is exactly where it was, only the style changed — instantly communicating what ControlNet does to a technical grader; (5) the kicker for this audience: an Isaac Sim robot-workcell render goes in, a photorealistic lab comes out through the same pipeline — a sim-to-real teaser that ties the course project back to the author's VLA research in one shot.

judge_verdicts.verdict: This is one of the rare proposals where both technologies are load-bearing: the LLM stack hits three named rubric techniques (prompt-engineered agent, RAG with the already-pulled embeddinggemma, schema-constrained structured output) and is the sole author of prompts/settings, while the diffusion stack natively covers ControlNet, LoRA, and inference acceleration inside a custom diffusers pipeline with correct low-level details (fp16-fix VAE, trailing-timestep Euler, guidance 1.0-1.5 with Lightning, LoRA unfuse for HQ). The deliverables story is complete and explicitly scheduled (Day 4 = README/architecture, requirements.txt, agent log, demo video), and the VRAM phase-separation plan shows the author actually understood the shared-GPU constraint rather than hand-waving it. Docked from 9 because the concept is a known archetype (RoomGPT-style restyling) and the LLM occupies the most common bolt-on role — upstream prompt author — with hand-written fallback templates that quietly admit the app survives without it.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Keep the LLM in the loop after generation, not just before it: add an iterative refine cycle where the user says "warmer / less cluttered / more brutalist," the LLM patches the validated JSON spec (delta-editing prompt, negative prompt, and conditioning scale) and regenerates — and surface the retrieved RAG passages as visible citations on the rationale card. This converts the LLM from a one-shot prompt-writer (the pattern graders penalize as near-decorative) into a stateful design agent, and makes the RAG verifiably real rather than claimed.

judge_verdicts.verdict: This is one of the rare proposals where every load-bearing claim checks out under verification: all seven HF models exist (including the exact Lightning 4-step LoRA filename), gemma4:e4b and embeddinggemma are already pulled locally, the VRAM arithmetic is correct down to the 0.8GB desktop overhead, and the central LLM/SDXL contention problem is solved with the right model choice plus keep_alive=0 and a CPU-offload fallback. Zero training, mature libraries on Python 3.13, ~15GB of downloads against 236GB free, and a scope-cut plan that protects a working core by end of Day 2. The only inflation is latency: the "~8s grid / ~2s per image" numbers are 4090-class, not 3090-class — expect 12-20s for four ControlNet images at 1024² — but the demo still feels live and Day 1 AM explicitly benchmarks this.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Re-baseline the speed claims for a 3090 — budget ~3-5s/image for SDXL+ControlNet at 4 steps (12-20s for the 2x2 grid, not 8s), decide batch-4 vs sequential generation from the Day-1 VRAM/latency measurement, and pin the Gradio version to one with native gr.ImageSlider (Gradio is now 6.x with breaking changes from the proposed 5.x).

judge_verdicts.verdict: This demos very well: the geometry-locked before/after slider is a true visceral wow moment, the 8-second 4-style grid is fast enough to run live, and the Isaac Sim sim-to-photoreal kicker is a memorable, audience-specific closer. It loses points because AI room restyling via depth ControlNet is one of the most cloned SDXL demos in existence (RoomGPT, InteriorAI, countless HF Spaces), so the core visual will read as familiar rather than novel to a generative-models classroom, and the 3-4 turn chat interview is the least watchable part of a tight 2-minute video.
judge_verdicts.improvement: Promote the Isaac Sim sim-to-photoreal restyler from a Day-3 bonus tab to a co-headline feature of the core demo (e.g., 'restyle any scene — your apartment or your robot's simulator'), since it is the only beat no other student or existing product can show; the interior-design half alone risks reading as a RoomGPT clone with better prompting.
